addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
Reacquainted. Here's where things stand:

## Repo state
- **App repo**: clean working copy — empty `@` on top of `main` at `c18ee719` *"Rename rdwr_vn100 to rw-vn100"*. No work in progress on disk.
- **`.claude` repo**: only the live session jsonl (normal during a session).
- **src/ layout**: refactor is landed — `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, `proto.rs`, `transact.rs`, `cli.rs`, `bench.rs` (431 lines).

## Active cycle: `0.3.0` — *feat: passive bench, composable command grammar*
Redesign `bench` to be purely **passive** (measure the live stream, no device mutation), decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a whole CLI line over **one connection**, and add file-backed named states. Design in `chores-01` [[1]].

**Ladder progress:**
- ✅ 0.3.0-0 prep (design note)
- ✅ 0.3.0-1 refactor → `proto.rs`
- ✅ 0.3.0-2 refactor → `transact.rs`
- ✅ 0.3.0-3 refactor → `cli.rs` + scaffold `bench.rs`
- ▶️ **0.3.0-4 (current) passive bench** — `bench [SECS]` measures the live stream only; drop configure/measure/restore; ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via `0xFA` sniff (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Lands in `bench.rs`.
- ⬜ 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs (`get-ascii`/`set-ascii` reg 6, `get-bin`/`set-bin` reg 75)
- ⬜ 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection
- ⬜ 0.3.0-7 named states (`--config` TOML, save/set/restore-state)
- ⬜ 0.3.0 close-out (README + `--help`, cargo cycle, version bump)

**Next action** is step **0.3.0-4** (passive `bench`). Want me to start on it, or did you have something else in mind?

message.content:
Good news I actually see running at 200hz with two fields at 115200:
nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-27T22:01:39.024Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)
$ rw-vn100 bench --bin --hz 200 --fields accel,gyro
rw-vn100 0.3.0-3.1
Opening /dev/ttyAMA0 at 115200 baud...
TX: $VNRRG,07*74
TX: $VNWRG,07,0*6D
TX: $VNWRG,75,2,4,01,0120*70
Configured binary output: Common["gyro", "accel"] @ 200 Hz (divisor 4, 30 B/frame).
Measuring for 5s...

Result: 1000 frames in 5.00s = 200.0 Hz (target 200 Hz).
Sample: gyro=[0.0023, 0.0018, -0.0029] rad/s, accel=[-10.066, -1.894, 0.941] m/s^2
Wire throughput ~60 kbit/s = 52% of the 115.2 kbit/s 115200-baud link.
TX: $VNWRG,75,0,4,01,0120*72
TX: $VNWRG,07,40*59
Restored: binary output off, ASCII async back to 40 Hz.
nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-27T22:01:57.138Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)
$ vi notes/todo.md 
nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-27T22:11:41.200Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)
$ rw-vn100 bench --bin --fields accel,gyro
rw-vn100 0.3.0-3.1
Opening /dev/ttyAMA0 at 115200 baud...
TX: $VNRRG,07*74
TX: $VNWRG,07,0*6D
TX: $VNWRG,75,2,20,01,0120*46
Configured binary output: Common["gyro", "accel"] @ 40 Hz (divisor 20, 30 B/frame).
Measuring for 5s...

Result: 200 frames in 5.01s = 39.9 Hz (target 40 Hz).
Sample: gyro=[-0.0002, -0.0035, 0.0013] rad/s, accel=[-10.034, -1.953, 0.955] m/s^2
Wire throughput ~12 kbit/s = 10% of the 115.2 kbit/s 115200-baud link.
TX: $VNWRG,75,0,20,01,0120*44
TX: $VNWRG,07,40*59
Restored: binary output off, ASCII async back to 40 Hz.
nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-27T22:12:34.367Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)

content.text:
That's the validation that matters — the 200 Hz goal is proven on real hardware, not just in theory:

- **1000 frames in 5.00s = 200.0 Hz exactly**, with both accel and gyro. No dropped frames, clean lock to the target.
- **Headroom is comfortable**: 30 B/frame × 200 Hz × 10 bits (8N1) = 60 kbit/s = 52% of the 115.2 link. The math checks out, and you're only at half the link — room for more fields or higher rate before 115200 becomes the wall.
- Sample values look sane: accel ≈ [-10.07, -1.89, 0.94] m/s² (≈1 g magnitude, mostly on -X), gyro near zero at rest.

This is exactly the binary-at-115200 path the project memory had as "proven, fc.py patch next" — now confirmed live with two fields at the full 200 Hz.

One thing this run also illustrates, relevant to the active cycle: the version is `0.3.0-3.1`, and you can see it's still the **old mutating bench** — `TX: $VNWRG,07,0` / `$VNWRG,75,...` configure, measure, then `Restored: binary output off`. That configure→measure→restore is precisely what **0.3.0-4 (passive bench)** removes. So the redesign's job is to give you these same numbers by *sniffing the live stream* the device is already emitting, without touching its config.

A question that bears on 0.3.0-4's design: in your two runs the bench *set* the rate (200 Hz, then 40 Hz) and then measured. Passive bench won't do that — it'll just report whatever the device is currently streaming. Does that match how you want to use it (configure once with `set-bin`/`set-hz`, then `bench` repeatedly to watch), or did you find the all-in-one configure-and-measure convenient enough to want to keep as a composed line later (0.3.0-6)?

Want me to start on 0.3.0-4, or were you just sharing the result?

message.content: Well the last message from the 200hz run was "Restored: binary output off, ASCII async back to 40 Hz." so the 40hz was expected. Our current design is the user needs to use save/restore if they want it restored to a "current" value. Do we agree.

content.text: Let me check the design note so I'm agreeing to what's actually written, not just nodding.

content.content:
71:## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar
76:(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
79:die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
86:  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
87:  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
122:  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
123:  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
124:  restore, all on one open.
145:- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
147:- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
149:- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
151:- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
152:  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
153:  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
154:  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
157:  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
194:- `bench.rs` — the `bench` command (passive, built in 0.3.0-4).
358:- After: read reg 7 → write reg 7=0 → write reg 75, and restore reg

key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

lastPrompt: Well the last message from the 200hz run was "Restored: binary output off, ASCII async back to 40 Hz." so the 40hz was expected. Our current design is the user needs to use save/restore if they want i…

content.text:
Agreed — and the design note backs it exactly.

From chores-01 [[1]] (lines 151–155):
- `default` is **never auto-applied**. "The tool stays passive by default; nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state` verb asks."

So under the redesign there's no implicit remember-and-put-back. If the user wants the device returned to its prior ("current") state, they make it explicit:
- `save-state` captures the current *device* regs (reg 6/7/75) up front, and
- `restore-state` writes them back at the end —

both composable on one open, e.g. the chores-01 example (lines 122–124): `save-state set-hz=50+bench ... set-hz=200+bench restore-state`.

This is precisely the contrast with what your two runs showed: the old `0.3.0-3` bench auto-restored ("Restored: binary output off, ASCII async back to 40 Hz") — that automatic mutation is the surprise-path the design deliberately drops (Issue #1 [[1]]). The new passive `bench` will leave the device exactly as it found it; restoring becomes the user's explicit choice via save/restore.

One boundary worth keeping in view: baud (reg 5) is excluded from restore by design (line 156–159) — `--baud` controls the link instead. So "restore to current value" means output config, not the link rate.

So yes, we agree.

lastPrompt: Why exclude baud from save and restore, it seems useful although how does it relate to --persist and what can be persisted?

content.text:
Pulled the facts. Here's the full picture — they're three separate mechanisms operating at different layers, which is the key to the whole question.

## Two independent axes

**`--persist` (VNWNV) — device RAM vs flash.** The VN-100 holds every register in volatile RAM; a `VNWRG` write takes effect immediately but reverts on power cycle. `--persist` issues a `VNWNV` ("write non-volatile") that commits the *current register bank* to flash so it survives a power cycle (lib.rs:131, lib.rs:185). So `--persist` is about **device durability across power cycles**.

**save/restore-state — host-side snapshot.** This is a *tool* concept (a TOML profile, ladder 0.3.0-7): the host reads some device regs into a named profile and can write them back later. It's about **the tool reapplying a value across invocations**.

These are orthogonal. You can `restore-state` a volatile profile (RAM only) or, if `--persist` is also given, commit it to flash. One is "what the device remembers"; the other is "what the tool remembers."

**What `--persist` actually persists:** `VNWNV` commits the *entire* current RAM register set to flash, not just the field you touched. The tool only exposes the flag on `set-hz` and `baud` today (cli.rs:168,172); the redesign extends it to the new `set-*` verbs (chores-01 line 165–166).

## Why baud (reg 5) is excluded from save/restore

Two distinct reasons, both in chores-01 (156–159) and borne out by the SetBaud code:

- **Rewriting baud is the wedge trigger itself.** Issue #1 [[1]]: a baud rewrite/reconnect at the link layer is exactly what wedges the UART until power cycle. save/restore exists to be a *safe* snapshot-and-reapply of output config. If `restore-state` silently wrote reg 5, every restore would roll the wedge die — defeating the passive-and-safe intent.
- **Baud is the link, not output config.** The tool's own connection rides on the baud. To "restore" a different baud, the open port has to follow it mid-restore — the delicate in-session switch dance (lib.rs:148–169, the 60 ms settle + buffer clear + verify). That's a fundamentally different and riskier operation than writing reg 6/7/75.

## On "it seems useful"

It looks useful but buys almost nothing once you trace it through:

- To even *talk* to the device, your connection is already open at its current baud — so a captured baud is, in the common case, the baud you're already at. Restoring it is a no-op write that still pays the wedge risk.
- If the device were at a *different* baud, you couldn't have connected to read the snapshot in the first place without already knowing/using that baud.

The legitimate "I want to control the link rate" need is already covered, and more safely, by the explicit surface: `--baud` to pick the rate for *this* connection, and `baud <N> --persist` once to make the device boot there. Baud changes stay deliberate and standalone, never a side effect of a restore.

So the split is: **output config (reg 6/7/75) → save/restore** (safe, the "current value" you actually want back); **link baud (reg 5) → `--baud` + explicit `baud` command** (the dangerous op, kept explicit). `--persist` is the perpendicular "make it stick across power cycles" flag that can apply to either.

One small consistency note: `save-state` doesn't just decline to *restore* baud — it doesn't *capture* it either (it snapshots reg 6/7/75 only, line 145–146, 156). That's deliberate: capturing baud would only tempt a restore of it.

Does that match your intent, or are you thinking of a use case where restoring the link rate is genuinely wanted (e.g. a profile that means "put the whole device, link included, into config X")?

message.content: So we need to be clear what save and restore saves/restores in a file local to the computer, totally different from persist which according to the help is hz and baud.

content.text:
Yes — agreed, and stated precisely they're two different storage locations entirely:

| Mechanism | Lives where | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **save / restore-state** | **host file** (the `--config` TOML profile map, on this computer) | device-output config registers |
| **`--persist` (VNWNV)** | **device flash** (on the VN-100 itself) | commits current RAM regs so they survive a power cycle |

So save/restore never touches the device's flash, and persist never touches a host file. The only thing they have in common is that they both can move *register values* around — but in different directions and at different layers:

- save/restore: **host file ↔ device RAM**
- persist: **device RAM → device flash**

## What save/restore actually covers (the "be clear" part)

The profile stores the **output config** registers, which is where the per-register split gets sharp:

| Register | save/restore (host file) | `--persist` (device flash) |
|---|---|---|
| reg 7 — async rate (Hz) | ✅ yes | ✅ yes (`set-hz --persist`) |
| reg 6 — ASCII async type | ✅ yes | via VNWNV, but no tool flag on `set-ascii` yet |
| reg 75 — binary output | ✅ yes | via VNWNV, but no tool flag on `set-bin` yet |
| reg 5 — **baud** | ❌ **no** | ✅ yes (`baud --persist`) |

Two things worth noting from this matrix:

1. **hz (reg 7) is the overlap** — it's the one field that's both in a save/restore profile *and* exposed to `--persist` today. So "persist according to the help is hz and baud" is right (cli.rs:168,172), but hz is the field that lives in *both* worlds, while baud lives *only* in persist's world.
2. **baud is the deliberate asymmetry** — it's persist-able (the device can boot at it) but never in a save/restore profile, for the wedge/link reasons we covered.

There's also a real design knob implied here: per chores-01 line 165–166, the redesign has `restore-state` apply **volatile by default, with `--persist` available** — i.e. `--persist` becomes a *modifier* you can stack on a restore to also commit it to flash. That's exactly the bridge between the two columns: restore reads the host file → writes device RAM → (optionally) VNWNV to flash.

This clarity isn't fully written down anywhere yet — chores-01 has the rules scattered across lines 145–159 and 165, and the `--help`/README only describe `--persist`. Want me to capture this two-axis model (host-file save/restore vs device-flash persist, plus the register matrix) as a `###` subsection in chores-01 so it's the single clear reference before 0.3.0-7 is coded? That's a notes-only edit — I'll show it first.

42:| OFF | 0 | async off |
60:## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD Reg 7; vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
64:## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §2; vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
65:Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
75:| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
83:| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |
86:Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.

file.content:
# VN-100 protocol reference (tool-scoped)

The authoritative values `rw-vn100` relies on, with citations. **Scope:** only
what this tool touches — not the whole VN-100 protocol. For anything else, go to
the primary sources directly.

**Verified 2026-06-21 against:**
- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §1.4

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included). Default on the UART. (Register 30 can
  switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message:** `0xFA | groups | <16-bit field mask per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  Uses a **16-bit CRC** regardless of Register 30. The CRC covers everything
  **after** the sync byte; running the CRC over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0**
  for a valid frame. Algorithm: CRC-16/CCITT (VectorNav app-note routine). ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD Reg 5; vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

content.text:
That's a documentation-source distinction, not a discrepancy — and it's worth being precise about, since 0.3.0-4's passive sniff depends on 0xFA being real.

**0xFA is an ICD detail, not a User Manual one.** Our `REFERENCE.md` cites the binary sync byte to **ICD §1.4** (Framing & checksums, line 22, 27):
> `0xFA | groups | <16-bit field mask per group> | payload | CRC16`

and it's verified (line 7–8) against `VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` — the **ICD v3.1.0.0** (2024-02-27), which is a *different document* from the **Rev 1.2.8 User Manual** you're reading. VectorNav splits these: the User Manual is the higher-level guide; the binary output framing (sync byte, group header, field mask, CRC16) lives in the ICD / the binary-protocol section. So "can't find 0xFA in the User Manual" is expected — wrong book, and also a different version line (ICD 3.x vs UM 1.2.8).

**It's also empirically confirmed, not just doc-sourced.** The `✓` on that REFERENCE.md line (line 17–18 defines it: "decoded correctly on real hardware") means we've proven it live — and you just re-proved it: your 200 Hz run decoded `gyro`/`accel` out of the binary stream, which the parser can only do by locking onto `0xFA` then CRC-checking (bench.rs:16, 120–133). A wrong sync byte would have yielded zero frames, not 1000-in-5.00s. So beyond any manual, the hardware says the sync byte is 0xFA.

**Honesty on my evidence chain:** I'm reading *our* REFERENCE.md (which cites the ICD and is marked hardware-confirmed) plus the working parser — I have **not** opened the ICD PDF or your Rev 1.2.8 User Manual myself this session. 

I can verify directly if you want — let me check whether the ICD PDF is actually reachable on this machine:

So, honest bottom line on your question:

- **0xFA is correct, and it's in the ICD §1.4, not the Rev 1.2.8 User Manual** — different VectorNav document. That's why your search came up empty.
- The strongest *independent* evidence available right now isn't a doc citation at all — it's the **live hardware**: your binary runs decode accel/gyro at the right rate, which only happens if the frame parser correctly syncs on 0xFA and the CRC zeroes out. That's the empirical `✓`.
- If you want a direct ICD page reference, point me at the PDF (or its path on this box) and I'll open it and quote the section verbatim.

One concrete suggestion that ties back to the skepticism: it might be worth a one-line note in REFERENCE.md recording that 0xFA / binary framing is an **ICD** detail (and *absent from the User Manual*), so the next person looking in the UM doesn't hit the same dead end. Notes-only — I'd show the diff first. Want it?

content.content:
1.4      CHECKSUM
All communication interfaces provide the option for utilizing an 8-bit XOR checksum, a 16-bit CRC checksum, or no
checksum, as configured in Register 30. By default, the UART serial interface utilizes an 8-bit checksum, whereas
the SPI bus utilizes no checksum. The 16-bit CRC checksum is recommended when high reliability is required.
For ASCII messages over the UART interface, the checksum is calculated over all bytes between, but not including,
the dollar sign ($) and asterisk (*). All delimiters (commas) are included in the checksum calculation. For messages
over the SPI bus, all bytes in the message, including the header but excluding the checksum itself, are included in
the checksum calculation.
The binary output messages sent over the UART serial interface utilize a 16-bit checksum regardless of the config-
uration in Register 30. See Section 1.1.4 for details on which bytes are included when calculating the checksum of
binary output messages.
1.4.1 Checksum Bypass
When communicating with the sensor over the UART interface, the checksum can be bypassed by replacing the
hexadecimal digits in the checksum with uppercase X characters. This works for both the XOR and CRC checksum
and allows for rapid development and testing through a serial terminal without requiring the checksum to be disabled.
An example command to read Register 1 over the UART interface using the checksum bypass feature is shown below.
     $VNRRG,1*XX
1.4.2    8-bit XOR Checksum
The 8-bit XOR checksum is a sequential XOR of each byte. The resultant checksum is an 8-bit number and is repre-
sented in the UART ASCII commands as two hexadecimal characters. The C function snippet below calculates the
correct checksum.

 8-bit XOR Checksum Calculation C-code Snippet
 // Calculates the 8-bit XOR checksum for the given byte sequence .
 unsigned char calculateChecksum ( unsigned char data [], unsigned int length )
 {
     unsigned int i;
     unsigned char cksum = 0;

        for(i=0; i< length ; i++){
            cksum ^= data[i];
        }

        return cksum;
 }

1.4.3    16-bit CRC Checksum
For cases where the 8-bit XOR checksum does not provide sufficient error detection, a full 16-bit CRC checksum is
available. The VN-100 uses the CRC16-CCITT algorithm. The resultant CRC checksum is a 16-bit number and is
represented in the UART ASCII commands as four hexadecimal characters. The C function snippet below calculates
the correct CRC.




13                               VectorNav Proprietary & Confidential Information         BASIC COMMUNICATION
 16-bit CRC Checksum Calculation C-code Snippet
// Calculates the 16- bit CRC checksum for the given byte sequence .
unsigned short calculateCRC ( unsigned char data [], unsigned int length )
{
  unsigned int i;

For cases where the 8-bit XOR checksum does not provide sufficient error detection, a full 16-bit CRC checksum is
available. The VN-100 uses the CRC16-CCITT algorithm. The resultant CRC checksum is a 16-bit number and is
represented in the UART ASCII commands as four hexadecimal characters. The C function snippet below calculates
the correct CRC.

content.content:
which allows configuring up to three different messages, each with customizable content and output rates. See
Appendix A.1 for examples of how to configure the desired binary output.
2.1.3 Message Format
A binary output message consists of four (4) components:
  1. Sync Byte The sync byte is the first byte of a binary output message and can be used to align to the output
     messages and has a value of 0xFA.
  2. Header The header starts with a binary group byte denoting the Binary Groups included in the message. The
     group byte is followed by a two-byte type word for each active group. See Table 2.1 for the group byte bit



                                  VectorNav Proprietary & Confidential Information                                  16
         offsets for each Binary Group. The remaining sections of this chapter detail the bit offsets and other details
        for each Binary Type in each group.
     3. Payload The payload consists of the output data selected based upon the bits selected in the group byte and
        type word. All output data in the payload section is ordered sequentially based first on the Binary Group bit
        offset, then by the Binary Type bit offset. As such, all Binary Types selected for a the lowest numbered Binary
        Group are output, followed by the Binary Types for the next Binary Group, etc. All data is directly concatenated,
        with no padding bytes between Binary Types.
     4. Checksum The checksum on all binary output messages is a 16-bit CRC checksum as defined in Section 1.4.3.
        The checksum is calculated over the header and the payload. As described in Section 1.4.3, the CRC checksum
        is output in big-endian format, such that if on receipt of a binary output message, the checksum itself is included
        in the checksum computation, a valid packet will produce a CRC checksum value of 0x0000. This provides
        a simple way of detecting packet corruption by simply checking to see if the CRC calculation of the entire
        message (not including the sync byte) results in zero.

           Bit Field: Binary Groups
             NAME          OFFSET            DESCRIPTION
                                             Binary group 1 contains a wide assortment of commonly used data re-
             Common              0
                                             quired for most applications.
             Time                1           The time group provides all timing and event counter related outputs.
                                             The IMU group provides all outputs which are dependent upon the mea-
             Imu                 2
                                             surements collected from the IMU.
                                             The attitude group provides all estimated outputs which are dependent
             Attitude            4
                                             upon the estimated attitude solution.
                                                                                                           TABLE 2.1

Figure 2.1 shows the format of a generic binary output message that contains data from N different groups with a
total payload length of M bytes. In most cases, total payload length M can be readily computed by summing the
fixed length of each configured binary output type. A table of binary output type sizes is provided in Table 2.2.
Note that some Binary Types may be defined as variable length. When a type has a variable length, it contains a
count or length variable that must be parsed to determine the length of that output type and the output message as
a whole. See Appendix A.1 for examples of the binary output message format.

              Binary Output Message Format
                                     Sync                                  Header
                                              Group      Type Bytes      Type Bytes                Type Bytes
                                              Bytes        Group A         Group B
                                                                                         …          Group X
                   Byte Offset         0        1        2         3     4         5     …        2N      2N+1
                   Type              uint8    uint8         uint16         uint16        …           uint16

                                                              Payload                             Checksum
                                        Outputs           Outputs                  Outputs

lastPrompt: I copied docs/ over from 3900x it's here now and I do see the 0xFA the first reference is in "2.1.3 Message Format" sub-section "1. Sync Byte .. 0xFA."

content.text:
Confirmed against the actual ICD PDF — you're correct, and our REFERENCE.md mis-cites it. Here's exactly what the ICD says:

**§2.1.3 Message Format** (where you found it) defines the binary message as four components:
> 1. **Sync Byte** ... "has a value of **0xFA**."
> 2. Header — group byte + a two-byte type word per active group
> 3. Payload
> 4. Checksum — "16-bit CRC checksum **as defined in Section 1.4.3**" ... "CRC calculation of the entire message (**not including the sync byte**) results in zero."

**§1.4 CHECKSUM** is *only* about checksums — §1.4.1 bypass, §1.4.2 the 8-bit XOR (ASCII), §1.4.3 the 16-bit CRC (CRC16-CCITT). It says nothing about the sync byte or the message layout.

So the split is:
- **Binary message format / 0xFA sync byte / group+type header / payload** → **§2.1.3** (not §1.4)
- **8-bit XOR checksum (ASCII)** → §1.4.2
- **16-bit CRC (CRC16-CCITT)** → §1.4.3

Our `REFERENCE.md` (line 22) heads the whole section "ICD **§1.4**" and parks the binary-message layout bullet under it — so the *substance* of line 27 is right (your live decode and the "CRC over everything-but-sync == 0" both check out against §2.1.3), but the **citation is wrong**: the framing belongs to §2.1.3, only the CRC algorithm belongs to §1.4.

There's a parallel mis-citation in code: `src/proto.rs:184` comments "Framing & checksums (ICD §1.4)" for the same thing.

## Bonus finding relevant to 0.3.0-4

§2.1.3 also documents **split packets**, whose sync byte is **0xFB**, not 0xFA (ICD line ~3720). Our passive sniff keys strictly on 0xFA (`bench.rs:16`), so it would silently skip any split packet. We think split packets won't appear for our small Common-group serial config, but it's a real caveat the passive-bench design should at least record.

## Proposed fix (notes/doc + one code comment — showing first, not applying)

In `REFERENCE.md`, split the citation:

```
## Framing & checksums — ICD §1.4.2/§1.4.3 (checksums), §2.1.3 (binary message format)

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included; ICD §1.4.2). Default on the UART.
  (Register 30 can switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message** (ICD §2.1.3): `0xFA | groups | <16-bit type word per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  The 0xFA **sync byte** and the group/type header are §2.1.3; a *split* packet
  uses sync `0xFB` instead. The trailing **16-bit CRC** is used regardless of
  Register 30 (ICD §1.4.3, CRC16-CCITT). The CRC covers everything **after** the
  sync byte; running it over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).
```

and fix the `§1.4` → `§2.1.3` reference in `src/proto.rs:184`.

Want me to apply that? And separately — now that docs/ is on this box, do you want me to audit the *rest* of REFERENCE.md's citations against the ICD while I'm in here (e.g. proto.rs:17 cites "§2.2 Table 2.3" for the Common group, but the Binary Groups bit field I just read is Table 2.1 — worth checking)?

message.content: Apply the fix and audit the rest. It's weird the formal checksum algorithm is in User Manual and not here and no mention of the ICD, and dumb people like me missed it :)

41	|---|---|---|

44	| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
45	| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |

# VN-100 protocol reference (tool-scoped)

The authoritative values `rw-vn100` relies on, with citations. **Scope:** only
what this tool touches — not the whole VN-100 protocol. For anything else, go to
the primary sources directly.

**Verified 2026-06-21 against:**
- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §1.4

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included). Default on the UART. (Register 30 can
  switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message:** `0xFA | groups | <16-bit field mask per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  Uses a **16-bit CRC** regardless of Register 30. The CRC covers everything
  **after** the sync byte; running the CRC over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0**
  for a valid frame. Algorithm: CRC-16/CCITT (VectorNav app-note routine). ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD Reg 5; vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD Reg 7; vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §2; vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1 ($VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Commands (no register) — ICD command section
- `$VNWNV` — Write Settings (save all registers to non-volatile flash).
- `$VNRST` — Reset (reboot; reloads flash).
- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot).
- `$VNRRG,<id>` / `$VNWRG,<id>,…` — generic Read/Write Register (`rrg` / `wrg`).

content.content:
===== §1.5 System Error Codes (528-575) =====
 1.5     SYSTEM ERROR CODES
In the event of an error, the VN-100 will output $VNERR, followed by an error code and the appropriate checksum. The
possible error codes are listed in Table 1.6 with a description of the error.

      Error Codes
       ERROR                      CODE     DESCRIPTION
                                           If this error occurs, then the firmware on the VN-100 has experi-
                                           enced a hard fault exception. Any asynchronous streaming is dis-
       Hard Fault                   01     abled, and requests to read measurement registers return Invalid
                                           Register errors. Sensor must be reset or power-cycle to resume
                                           operation (if possible).
                                           The processor’s serial input buffer has experienced an overflow.
       Serial Buffer Overflow       02
                                           The processor has a 256 character input buffer.
       Invalid Checksum             03     The checksum for the received command was invalid.
       Invalid Command              04     The user has requested an invalid command.
                                           The user did not supply the minimum number of required param-
       Not Enough Parameters        05
                                           eters for the requested command.
                                           The user supplied too many parameters for the requested com-
       Too Many Parameters          06
                                           mand.
                                           The user supplied a parameter for the requested command which
       Invalid Parameter            07
                                           was invalid.
       Invalid Register             08     An invalid register was specified.
       Unauthorized Access          09     The user does not have permission to write to this register.
                                           A watchdog reset has occurred. In the event of a non-recoverable
       Watchdog Reset               0A     error the internal watchdog will reset the processor within 50 ms
                                           of the error.
                                           The output buffer has experienced an overflow. The processor
       Output Buffer Overflow       0B
                                           has a 2048 character output buffer.
                                           The baud rate is not high enough to support the requested asyn-
       Insufficient Baud Rate       0C
                                           chronous data output at the requested data rate.
       Error Buffer Overflow        FF     An overflow event has occurred on the system error buffer.
                                                                                                    TABLE 1.6




15                               VectorNav Proprietary & Confidential Information         BASIC COMMUNICATION
 2           BINARY OUTPUT MESSAGES

===== Reg6 ADOR default (1460-1478) =====
 3.2.3        Async Data Output Type
            Register ID . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 6
            Size (Bytes) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4
            Description . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Sets which ASCII measurement register is output as asynchronously.
            Example Read Response . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . $VNRRG,06,0*69
            Example Write Command . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . $VNWRG,06,1,1*XX

 OFFSET             NAME                              FORMAT                 UNIT                DEFAULT                 DESCRIPTION
    0               Ador                               uint32                ENUM                   14                   ASCII asynchronous data output register.
                                                                                                                         Optional. Asynchronous data type on serial
     4              SerialPort                            uint8              ENUM                        –               port. If this parameter is not provided then the
                                                                                                                         ADOR will be changed for the active serial port.


                    The serial port parameter in this register is optional. If it is not provided, the ADOR will be changed

        i           on the active serial port. The response to this register will include the serial port parameter if the
                    optional parameter is provided. If the second parameter is not provided, the response will not include
                    this parameter.

content.text: Audit verified — all *values* in REFERENCE.md are correct; the citation precision needs work, and one (framing) was outright wrong. Applying all fixes now.

input.old_string:
## Framing & checksums — ICD §1.4

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included). Default on the UART. (Register 30 can
  switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message:** `0xFA | groups | <16-bit field mask per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  Uses a **16-bit CRC** regardless of Register 30. The CRC covers everything
  **after** the sync byte; running the CRC over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0**
  for a valid frame. Algorithm: CRC-16/CCITT (VectorNav app-note routine). ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).
input.new_string:
## Framing & checksums — ICD §2.1.3 (binary message format), §1.4.2/§1.4.3 (checksums)

The binary **message format** (sync byte, group/type header, payload) is ICD
§2.1.3 — *not* §1.4, which covers only checksums. The **User Manual (UM001)
omits the binary framing entirely** and never points to the ICD, so the sync
byte is not findable there; go to the ICD.

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included; ICD §1.4.2). Default on the UART.
  (Register 30 can switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message** (ICD §2.1.3): `0xFA | groups | <16-bit type word per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  The `0xFA` **sync byte** is the first byte; a *split* packet uses sync `0xFB`
  instead (ICD §2.1.3, "split packet") — our passive sniff keys on `0xFA` only.
  The trailing **16-bit CRC** is used regardless of Register 30 (ICD §1.4.3,
  CRC16-CCITT). The CRC covers everything **after** the sync byte; running it
  over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# VN-100 protocol reference (tool-scoped)

The authoritative values `rw-vn100` relies on, with citations. **Scope:** only
what this tool touches — not the whole VN-100 protocol. For anything else, go to
the primary sources directly.

**Verified 2026-06-21 against:**
- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §1.4

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included). Default on the UART. (Register 30 can
  switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message:** `0xFA | groups | <16-bit field mask per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  Uses a **16-bit CRC** regardless of Register 30. The CRC covers everything
  **after** the sync byte; running the CRC over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0**
  for a valid frame. Algorithm: CRC-16/CCITT (VectorNav app-note routine). ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD Reg 5; vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD Reg 7; vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §2; vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1 ($VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Commands (no register) — ICD command section
- `$VNWNV` — Write Settings (save all registers to non-volatile flash).
- `$VNRST` — Reset (reboot; reloads flash).
- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot).
- `$VNRRG,<id>` / `$VNWRG,<id>,…` — generic Read/Write Register (`rrg` / `wrg`).

the primary sources directly.
-**Verified 2026-06-21 against:**
+**Verified 2026-06-27 against:**
The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`

- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §2.1.3 (binary message format), §1.4.2/§1.4.3 (checksums)

The binary **message format** (sync byte, group/type header, payload) is ICD
§2.1.3 — *not* §1.4, which covers only checksums. The **User Manual (UM001)
omits the binary framing entirely** and never points to the ICD, so the sync
byte is not findable there; go to the ICD.

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included; ICD §1.4.2). Default on the UART.
  (Register 30 can switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message** (ICD §2.1.3): `0xFA | groups | <16-bit type word per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  The `0xFA` **sync byte** is the first byte; a *split* packet uses sync `0xFB`
  instead (ICD §2.1.3, "split packet") — our passive sniff keys on `0xFA` only.
  The trailing **16-bit CRC** is used regardless of Register 30 (ICD §1.4.3,
  CRC16-CCITT). The CRC covers everything **after** the sync byte; running it
  over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD Reg 5; vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD Reg 7; vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §2; vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1 ($VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Commands (no register) — ICD command section
- `$VNWNV` — Write Settings (save all registers to non-volatile flash).
- `$VNRST` — Reset (reboot; reloads flash).
- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot).
- `$VNRRG,<id>` / `$VNWRG,<id>,…` — generic Read/Write Register (`rrg` / `wrg`).

lines: over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).
-## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD Reg 5; vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
+## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD §3.2.2 (Table 3.3); vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |

| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |

| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |

# VN-100 protocol reference (tool-scoped)

The authoritative values `rw-vn100` relies on, with citations. **Scope:** only
what this tool touches — not the whole VN-100 protocol. For anything else, go to
the primary sources directly.

**Verified 2026-06-27 against:**
- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §2.1.3 (binary message format), §1.4.2/§1.4.3 (checksums)

The binary **message format** (sync byte, group/type header, payload) is ICD
§2.1.3 — *not* §1.4, which covers only checksums. The **User Manual (UM001)
omits the binary framing entirely** and never points to the ICD, so the sync
byte is not findable there; go to the ICD.

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included; ICD §1.4.2). Default on the UART.
  (Register 30 can switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message** (ICD §2.1.3): `0xFA | groups | <16-bit type word per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  The `0xFA` **sync byte** is the first byte; a *split* packet uses sync `0xFB`
  instead (ICD §2.1.3, "split packet") — our passive sniff keys on `0xFA` only.
  The trailing **16-bit CRC** is used regardless of Register 30 (ICD §1.4.3,
  CRC16-CCITT). The CRC covers everything **after** the sync byte; running it
  over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD §3.2.2 (Table 3.3); vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD §3.2.4 (Table 3.9); vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |

| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).

## Error responses — ICD §1.5 (Table 1.6, $VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |

| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot).

content.text:
Verified — my REFERENCE.md note is accurate on all three points:

- **0xFA / sync byte in UM: 0 hits** — confirms your search; the UM genuinely doesn't have it.
- **ICD references in UM: none** — the UM never points the reader to the ICD.
- **Checksum algorithm IS in the UM** (§5.2 Checksum/CRC, §5.2.1.1) — exactly your observation. And the only "binary message format" the UM mentions is for the *SPI* interface (line 1185), not the UART binary output with 0xFA.

So this wasn't a "dumb" miss at all — the doc structure sets the trap: the UM gives you the checksum math (so it feels complete), omits the UART binary framing, and never cross-references the ICD. A UM-only reader *cannot* find 0xFA. That's a documentation gap on VectorNav's side, not a reader failing.

content.text:
Build clean. Here's the complete audit and what changed.

## Audit results — every REFERENCE.md citation vs. ICD v3.1.0.0

| Item | Old citation | ICD reality | Values | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Framing & checksums | §1.4 | format = **§2.1.3**; XOR = §1.4.2; CRC = §1.4.3 | ✓ correct | **WRONG cite → fixed** |
| Reg 5 Baud | "ICD Reg 5" | **§3.2.2** (Table 3.3) | all 9 values + default 115200 ✓ | imprecise → sharpened |
| Reg 6 ADOR | §3.2.3 (Table 3.6) | §3.2.3 (Table 3.6) | all 14 values + default 14 ✓ | ✅ already correct |
| Reg 7 ADOF | "ICD Reg 7" | **§3.2.4** (Table 3.9) | all 12 values + default 40, max 200 ✓ | imprecise → sharpened |
| Reg 75/76/77 | §2 | regs **§3.2.8–3.2.10**; msg fmt §2 | 800/rateDivisor ✓ (ICD line: divisor 20 → 40 Hz) | imprecise → sharpened |
| Common Group | §2.2 (Table 2.3) | §2.2 (Table 2.3) "Common OutputFields" | all 10 bit offsets ✓ | ✅ already correct |
| Sub-field sizes | §2.4 | §2.4 (IMU group: UncompAccel 2.4.3, UncompGyro 2.4.4, Pressure 2.4.6) | 12/12/4 ✓ | ✅ already correct |
| Error responses | §1 | **§1.5** (Table 1.6) | all 13 codes ✓ | imprecise → sharpened |
| Commands | "command section" | §1.3.3 WNV, §1.3.4 RFS, §1.3.5 RST, §1.3.1/.2 RRG/WRG | ✓ | imprecise → sharpened |

**Bottom line:** every *value* in REFERENCE.md was already correct against the ICD. Only the **framing citation was outright wrong** (§1.4 → §2.1.3); the rest were just imprecise (register-number or chapter-level) and are now sharpened to subsection + table.

## Files changed (working copy, uncommitted)

- **`REFERENCE.md`** — fixed framing section (§2.1.3 + §1.4.2/.3), added the UM-omits-binary-framing note + the split-packet `0xFB` caveat, sharpened reg 5 / 7 / 75 / error / command citations, bumped "Verified" date to 2026-06-27.
- **`src/proto.rs`** — `vn_crc16` cite §1.4 → §2.1.3/§1.4.3; `error_description` cite §1 → §1.5.
- **`src/cli.rs`** — `VALID_RATES` cite → §3.2.4; `VALID_BAUDS` cite → §3.2.2.

`cargo check` passes (comment-only changes).

Two follow-ups for your call:
1. **Commit?** This is a clean, self-contained doc/citation fix. It doesn't belong to the 0.3.0 bench ladder, so I'd suggest a small standalone commit (`docs: correct ICD citations in REFERENCE.md`) rather than folding it into the cycle — but I won't commit or push without your go-ahead.
2. The **split-packet `0xFB`** caveat is now recorded in REFERENCE.md; do you want a matching `notes/todo.md` entry so the passive-bench sniff (0.3.0-4) explicitly decides whether to handle or ignore split packets, or is "documented + ignore" good enough?
