toolUseResult.originalFile:
# VN-100 protocol reference (tool-scoped)

The authoritative values `rw-vn100` relies on, with citations. **Scope:** only
what this tool touches — not the whole VN-100 protocol. For anything else, go to
the primary sources directly.

**Verified 2026-06-27 against:**
- **ICD** — `../docs/VN100-ICD-v3_1_0_0-ICD10005-R1.pdf` (v3.1.0.0, R1, 2024-02-27).
  The authoritative protocol/register spec. Cited by **section number**.
- **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
  (vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
  (class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.

> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Framing & checksums — ICD §2.1.3 (binary message format), §1.4.2/§1.4.3 (checksums)

The binary **message format** (sync byte, group/type header, payload) is ICD
§2.1.3 — *not* §1.4, which covers only checksums. The **User Manual (UM001)
omits the binary framing entirely** and never points to the ICD, so the sync
byte is not findable there; go to the ICD.

- **ASCII command:** `$<payload>*XX\r\n`. `XX` is an **8-bit XOR** over every byte
  *between* `$` and `*` (commas included; ICD §1.4.2). Default on the UART.
  (Register 30 can switch checksum mode; this tool assumes the default.) ✓
- **Binary message** (ICD §2.1.3): `0xFA | groups | <16-bit type word per group> | payload | CRC16`.
  The `0xFA` **sync byte** is the first byte; a *split* packet uses sync `0xFB`
  instead (ICD §2.1.3, "split packet") — our passive sniff keys on `0xFA` only.
  The trailing **16-bit CRC** is used regardless of Register 30 (ICD §1.4.3,
  CRC16-CCITT). The CRC covers everything **after** the sync byte; running it
  over `groups…payload…CRC` yields **0** for a valid frame. ✓
- Code: `checksum()` / `verify_checksum()` (ASCII), `vn_crc16()` (binary).

## Register 5 — Serial Baud Rate — ICD §3.2.2 (Table 3.3); vnsdk `BaudRate::BaudRates`
`9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600`
Code: `VALID_BAUDS`. (Factory default 115200.)

## Register 6 — Async Data Output Type / ADOR — ICD §3.2.3 (Table 3.6); vnsdk `AsyncOutputType::Ador`
A **single** selection (one preset *or* off), not a bitmask. **Default = 14 (YMR).**

| ADOR | value | message (source register) |
|---|---|---|
| OFF | 0 | async off |
| YPR | 1 | Yaw,Pitch,Roll (reg 8) |
| QTN | 2 | Quaternion (reg 9) |
| QMR | 8 | Quat,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 15) |
| MAG | 10 | Magnetic (reg 17) |
| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD §3.2.4 (Table 3.9); vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1.5 (Table 1.6, $VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Commands (no register) — ICD §1.3
- `$VNWNV` — Write Settings (save all registers to non-volatile flash). §1.3.3
- `$VNRST` — Reset (reboot; reloads flash). §1.3.5
- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot). §1.3.4
- `$VNRRG,<id>` / `$VNWRG,<id>,…` — generic Read/Write Register (`rrg` / `wrg`). §1.3.1 / §1.3.2

lines: - **vnsdk** — `../vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/{Registers,Errors}.hpp`
(vnsdk **v1.2.0**, see `../vnsdk/changelog.txt`). Cited by **symbol name**
(class/enum), never line number — line numbers rot across SDK releases.
+- **User Manual** — `../docs/VN100-T_UserManual-UM001.pdf` (UM001, Rev 1.2.8,
+  **Firmware v1.1**). Higher-level guide for **older firmware than our device**
+  (see "Firmware version & document provenance" below) — used only where the ICD
+  is silent, and re-verified against the ICD or the live device.
> Citation form: `(ICD §X; vnsdk Class::Enum)`. When re-verifying against a newer
> ICD/SDK, update the "Verified" date above and any values that changed.

A ✓ means **empirically confirmed** on real hardware (a `bench` run decoded
correctly); otherwise the value is doc-confirmed from the sources above.

---

## Firmware version & document provenance

The VN-100 ships protocol docs **per firmware version**, and the two we have
disagree: the ICD is for Firmware **v3.1.0.0**, the User Manual for Firmware
**v1.1**. The tie-breaker is the device itself — read live from its
identification registers (`rw-vn100 rrg <id>`):

| reg | field | ICD § | our device |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Model Number | §4.2.1 | `VN-100S-CR` ✓ |
| 2 | Hardware Version | §4.2.2 | `7` ✓ |
| 3 | Serial Number | §4.2.3 | `0100130608` ✓ |
| 4 | Firmware Version | §4.2.4 | `3.1.0.0` ✓ |

- **Firmware 3.1.0.0 matches the ICD**, so the ICD is the authoritative match
  for this device — trust it first. Every register/enum value in this file is
  ICD-sourced and (where marked ✓) confirmed on this firmware.
- **The User Manual (UM001, Rev 1.2.8) targets Firmware v1.1** — two major
  versions behind. We think UM-sourced facts may not hold on firmware 3.1.0.0,
  so the UM is used only for what the ICD lacks, then re-verified.
- Re-check the device version any time with `rw-vn100 rrg 4` (UM §7.5 / ICD
  §4.2.4 document the same register). If a future device reports a different
  firmware, re-verify this whole file against the matching ICD.

---

| ACC | 11 | Acceleration (reg 18) |
| GYR | 12 | Angular Rate (reg 19) |
| MAR | 13 | Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 20) |
| YMR | 14 | YPR,Mag,Accel,Rates (reg 27) — **default** |
| YBA | 16 | YPR,Body Accel,Rates (reg 239) |
| YIA | 17 | YPR,Inertial Accel,Rates (reg 240) |
| IMU | 19 | IMU Measurements (reg 54) |
| DTV | 30 | Delta Theta & Delta Velocity (reg 80) |
| HVE | 34 | Heave (reg 115) |

Code: `ASCII_TYPES` (`bench --type`). The SDK also defines GPS/INS values
(GPS/GPE/INS/INE/ISL/ISE/G2S/G2E) — **not applicable to the VN-100** (no GNSS).

## Register 7 — Async Data Output Frequency / ADOF — ICD §3.2.4 (Table 3.9); vnsdk `AsyncOutputFreq::Adof`
`0(off), 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200` Hz. **Max 200.** ✓ (40 default)
Code: `VALID_RATES` (non-zero values; `set-hz` / ASCII `bench`).

## Registers 75/76/77 — Binary Output 1/2/3 — ICD §3.2.8–3.2.10 (registers), §2 (message format); vnsdk `BinaryOutput1/2/3`
Write fields: `asyncMode` (serial-port bitmask), `rateDivisor`, then a field mask
per selected group. **Output rate = 800 / rateDivisor** (800 Hz IMU base; so
`rateDivisor 4` → 200 Hz). ✓ Three independent outputs, each its own rate.

### Common Group (group byte `0x01`) — ICD §2.2 (Table 2.3)
Bit offsets within the Common field mask, and on-wire sizes:

| bit | field | content | bytes | tool name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TimeStartup | `u64` ns | 8 | `time` ✓ |
| 2 | TimeSyncIn | `u64` ns | 8 | — |
| 3 | Ypr | 3×`f32` deg | 12 | `ypr` ✓ |
| 4 | Quaternion | 4×`f32` | 16 | `quat` ✓ |
| 5 | AngularRate | 3×`f32` rad/s | 12 | `gyro` ✓ |
| 8 | Accel | 3×`f32` m/s² | 12 | `accel` ✓ |
| 9 | Imu | UncompAccel(12)+UncompGyro(12) | 24 | `imu` |
| 10 | MagPres | Mag(12)+Temp(4)+Pres(4) | 20 | `magpres` |
| 11 | Deltas | DeltaTheta+DeltaVel | 28 | — |
| 13 | SyncInCnt | `u32` | 4 | — |

Sub-field sizes per ICD §2.4 (UncompAccel/UncompGyro = 12 each; Pressure = 4).
Frame length = `1 (sync) + 1 (groups) + 2 (field mask) + payload + 2 (CRC)`.
Code: `FIELDS` (`bench --bin --fields`).

## Error responses — ICD §1.5 (Table 1.6, $VNERR); vnsdk `Errors.hpp` `Error`
`$VNERR,<code>*XX` — `<code>` is **hex**.

| code | meaning | | code | meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | hard fault | | 0x08 | invalid register |
| 0x02 | serial buffer overflow | | 0x09 | unauthorized access |
| 0x03 | invalid checksum | | 0x0A | watchdog reset |
| 0x04 | invalid command | | 0x0B | output buffer overflow |
| 0x05 | not enough parameters | | **0x0C** | **insufficient baud rate** |
| 0x06 | too many parameters | | 0xFF | error buffer overflow |
| 0x07 | invalid parameter | | | |

`0x0C` is the common one here: too much data for the current baud. Code:
`error_description()` / `vnerr_message()`.

## Commands (no register) — ICD §1.3
- `$VNWNV` — Write Settings (save all registers to non-volatile flash). §1.3.3
- `$VNRST` — Reset (reboot; reloads flash). §1.3.5
- `$VNRFS` — Restore Factory Settings (defaults + reboot). §1.3.4
- `$VNRRG,<id>` / `$VNWRG,<id>,…` — generic Read/Write Register (`rrg` / `wrg`). §1.3.1 / §1.3.2

- **New "Firmware version & document provenance" section** stating the core problem plainly: the ICD is for firmware **v3.1.0.0**, the UM for firmware **v1.1**, and the device itself (firmware **3.1.0.0** via `rrg 4`) is the tie-breaker — **matching the ICD**. - **Identification-register table** (reg 1–4 → ICD §4.2.1–4.2.4) with your live values, all ✓.
- **Sources block** now lists the User Manual with its firmware-v1.1 caveat.
- The "we think UM-sourced facts may not hold on firmware 3.1.0.0" claim carries the speculation marker per AGENTS.md (it's an inference, not measured).

## Full set of uncommitted working-copy changes now
- `REFERENCE.md` — framing citation fix, provenance/version section, UM source + caveat, sharpened reg/error/command citations, verified-date bump.
- `src/proto.rs`, `src/cli.rs` — matching citation fixes in doc comments.
- `cargo check` passes.

## Two things for your call

1. **Commit.** This is now a coherent standalone unit: *"docs: correct ICD citations + firmware-version provenance in REFERENCE.md"* (plus the two source-comment fixes). Independent of the 0.3.0 bench ladder. Want me to commit it on its own? (I won't push without the per-push approval.)

2. **A `version`/`info` command?** You just demonstrated that model/HW-rev/serial/firmware live in reg 1–4, and we now *depend* on `rrg 4` for the provenance check. A small `rw-vn100 version` (or `info`) that reads all four and prints them — plus a startup check that warns if firmware ≠ 3.1.0.0 — would make "is this device on the firmware our docs assume?" a one-liner instead of four `rrg` calls. Want me to add a `notes/todo.md` entry for it (not implement now), or is the raw `rrg` sufficient?

content.text: Let me check the actual CLI surface rather than assume.

input.command: grep -nE '"\$?VN|=> *Command::|Command::|--bin|--fields|--type|fields:|bin:' src/cli.rs | grep -iE 'command::|--bin|--fields|--type|fields:|bin:' | head -60
input.description: Find Command variants and --bin/--fields usage

content.text:
Confirmed — you're right. Here's the full command surface (cli.rs:101–137):

| Command | Touches | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| `bench` | reg 7 (ASCII) **or** reg 75 (binary) | **only command with `--bin`** / `--fields` / `--serial-port` |
| `get-hz` / `set-hz` | reg 7 — ASCII async rate | ASCII only |
| `baud` | reg 5 | link, n/a |
| `rrg` / `wrg` | any register (generic) | raw — no mode concept |
| `reset` / `factory-reset` | — | n/a |

So **`--bin` is exclusive to `bench`**. Everything else is either ASCII-async config (`get-hz`/`set-hz` = reg 7), the link baud, or raw register access. The *only* way to configure binary output (reg 75) outside `bench` today is hand-rolling a generic `wrg 75,2,4,01,0120` — no friendly verb for it.

And that's precisely the gap the active 0.3.0 cycle closes. From the ladder:

> **0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs:** add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii` (reg 6) and **`get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75)** beside the existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`.

So after 0.3.0-5, binary becomes a first-class, composable thing (`set-bin --fields accel,gyro`, `get-bin`) instead of a flag bolted onto `bench`. Note also that `bench`'s own `--bin` is itself slated to change: 0.3.0-4 makes `bench` **passive** (it'll measure whatever's streaming, ASCII or binary, without configuring), so the configure-via-`bench --bin` behavior you saw is on its way out — configuration moves to `set-bin`.

So the current ASCII-centric command set isn't the intended end state; it's mid-refactor, and binary parity is the next ladder rung.

content.text:
Not ignorance at all — the naming invites exactly this confusion. The answer is **no**, and the ICD makes the reason clean. There are two *independent* channels on the UART:

## 1. Command / register channel — always ASCII
Read/Write Register over the UART are **always** ASCII, regardless of any binary config (ICD §1.3.1/§1.3.2). Straight from the ICD:

```
$VNRRG,5*46              ← read reg 5
$VNRRG,5,115200*6D       ← ASCII reply
```

There's a binary opcode (`0x01`/`0x02`) listed next to each — but that's the **SPI bus**, a different physical interface. Over the UART, talking to registers is ASCII, full stop. Your own `rrg 1-4` run proves it: `$VNRRG,01,VN-100S-CR*35` came back ASCII.

## 2. Async output channel — ASCII *or* binary, your choice
This is the only place "binary" applies. The device's continuous measurement stream can be ASCII async (reg 6/7) **or** binary (reg 75). That's what `--bin` selects. It changes the *output stream format*, not how commands work.

## The key mental model
"Binary mode" isn't a global device state. It's just the format of channel 2. So:

- **You configure the binary stream using ASCII commands.** `set-bin` (0.3.0-5) will send `$VNWRG,75,...` — an ASCII command that turns on a binary *output*. No contradiction.
- The two channels run **simultaneously**: in your `bench --bin` run, the tool sent ASCII `$VNWRG,75,2,4,01,0120*70` and read ASCII replies *while* the device was streaming binary `0xFA` frames. ASCII commands + binary data, same wire, same time.

## Two footnotes (so the picture's complete)
- **SPI** is a fully-binary interface (everything is `0x01`/`0x02`/... opcodes) — but we don't use it; we're on the UART.
- **`$VNBOM` (Poll Binary Output, §1.3.11)** is the one hybrid: you send an *ASCII* command `$VNBOM,1*45` and the device replies with a *binary* frame `FA 01 29 01 ...` instead of ASCII. But that's a one-shot *poll of the output message*, not a register read — registers themselves never go binary on the UART.

So: switching to binary output never changes register reads/writes. They stay ASCII. The redesign's `get-bin`/`set-bin` are just ASCII reg-75 commands with a friendly face.

message.content:
More clarification, the device can always be streaming, so if we're in binary
mode and want to read a register do you stop the streaming write the request
then read response then start streaming again, or ...?

content.text: This is the crux of the whole single-connection redesign, so let me answer from what the tool actually does, not theory. Let me read the transaction code.

lastPrompt: More clarification, the device can always be streaming, so if we're in binary mode and want to read a register do you stop the streaming write the request then read response then start streaming again…

content.text:
Great question, and it's the heart of the single-connection redesign. The answer is the "or ...": **you don't stop streaming.** You leave it running and pull your ASCII response out of the mixed byte stream. Here's the grounding.

## What the tool actually does

The command/response and the async stream **share one UART, interleaved**. When you send `$VNRRG,7`, the device keeps streaming; its ASCII reply just appears amid the data. `read_reply` (transact.rs:18–78) demultiplexes:

1. It reads raw bytes, accumulating until a `\n` (transact.rs:51–53).
2. On `\n`, it takes the accumulated bytes and **slices from the last `$`** (transact.rs:59–62):
   ```rust
   let candidate = match raw.rfind('$') {
       Some(p) => raw[p..].trim().to_string(),   // ASCII reply starts at last '$'
       None    => raw.trim().to_string(),
   };
   ```
   Binary frame bytes sitting in front of the echo get sliced off.
3. It checks the candidate against the accept predicate, and `transact` then validates the checksum (transact.rs:96).

There's a test proving exactly your scenario (transact.rs:174–183): binary bytes `0xFA 01 10 99 00` immediately precede `$VNWRG,75,...\r\n`, and `read_reply` cleanly extracts the ASCII reply. Your own 200 Hz run did this live — the tool sent `$VNWRG,75,2,4,01,0120*70` *while* binary frames were streaming and read clean replies.

## So, of your three options

- **(A) Interleave — leave streaming on, demux the reply.** ✅ This is what the tool does. No device mutation, no gap in the stream.
- **(B) Quiesce — stop streaming, transact, restart.** The *old* bench did a version of this (set reg 7 = 0 before binary config), but that *mutates* device state and leaves a streaming gap — exactly the surprise-mutation the passive redesign is removing. Not the read strategy.
- **(C) Poll-only — don't stream, use `rrg`/`$VNBOM` on demand.** A different usage model entirely.

The passive philosophy makes (A) the right default: never mutate the device just to observe it.

## The honest caveat

The `rfind('$')` approach is a **heuristic, not a true frame-aware demultiplexer.** Binary payloads can legitimately contain `0x24` (`$`), `0x0D` (`\r`), `0x0A` (`\n`) bytes, so in principle a binary frame could inject a stray `\n` (splitting a line oddly) or a `$` (though `rfind` taking the *last* `$` means the real echo, which ends in `\r\n`, almost always wins). The defenses stack up:

- slice from the last `$`,
- `verify_checksum` rejects a mangled candidate,
- `transact_retry` (transact.rs:108) resends if a read fails.

At our rates this is reliable. But a truly bulletproof version would track `0xFA` frame lengths and only feed *non-frame* bytes to the ASCII line parser — i.e. parse the two interleaved protocols separately instead of leaning on `$`/`\n`. We think that's worth a `notes/` mention, because 0.3.0-6 ("step grammar + one connection") will run things like `set-bin+get-hz+bench` over one open port — i.e. reading registers *while binary streams* becomes a routine path, not just the disable-binary edge case the current test covers.

Want me to add that as a todo/backlog item (frame-aware demux for register reads during binary streaming), so the redesign decides explicitly whether the heuristic is good enough or needs hardening?

message.content: But to me, casually looking at the code, when we're reading a register response it looks like we're throwing away the streaming data, but could be wrong.

content.text:
You're not wrong — that's exactly what happens. I traced it to be sure.

## Where the stream data gets discarded

In `read_reply`, two paths throw away streaming data:

1. **The slice-from-last-`$`** (transact.rs:59–62). When a line completes, anything before the last `$` is dropped:
   ```rust
   Some(p) => raw[p..].trim().to_string(),   // everything before the echo's '$' is gone
   ```
   So binary frame bytes (or a partial async line) sitting in front of the register echo are discarded.

2. **The clear-on-non-match** (transact.rs:63–66). Every completed line is `line.clear()`'d, and only returned if it matches:
   ```rust
   line.clear();
   if matches(&candidate) { return Ok(Some(candidate)); }
   // ...otherwise loop continues; that line is just gone
   ```
   So an async ASCII line like `$VNYMR,...` that arrives while we're waiting for `$VNRRG,07` doesn't match → silently dropped. Binary frames that happen to contain a `0x0A` get a spurious line break, fail to match, and are dropped too.

Net: while a register transaction is in flight, **all concurrent stream data — binary frames and async ASCII alike — is read off the wire and thrown on the floor.** `read_reply` returns *only* the one matching reply.

## Does it matter?

For the current and intended usage, no — and here's the distinction that makes it safe:

- **Register transactions** (`rrg`, `set-hz`, `get-bin`, …) only want the register value. Discarding the stream that flows past during those few hundred milliseconds is exactly right — we have no use for it.
- **Measurement** uses a *different code path*. `bench` doesn't call `read_reply`; it has its own loop (bench.rs) that counts frames/lines and resyncs on `0xFA`. So measurement accuracy isn't touched by `read_reply`'s discarding.

The one real boundary effect: after a register read returns, the device's binary stream is mid-frame, so the first bytes the measurement loop sees may be a partial frame — but the loop resyncs on the next `0xFA`, so it self-heals (one frame lost at most).

## The design implication for 0.3.0-6

This is fine **as long as register transactions and the measurement window don't overlap** — which the planned step model guarantees, since `set-bin`, `get-hz`, and `bench` are *separate sequential steps* on the one connection, not concurrent. Each register step eats a sliver of stream, then `bench` measures its own clean window.

It would *only* become a problem if you ever wanted to read a register **without** interrupting a continuous measurement — e.g. a long-running logger that also polls a register. Then you'd need a single shared reader that **demultiplexes** both protocols (route `0xFA` frames to the frame counter, `$VN...` lines to the command matcher) instead of one that keeps the reply and discards the rest. The current code can't do that; it's a "transact *or* measure," not "both at once."

So your instinct is correct, and it points at the same gap as my earlier note: the tool today treats command-response and streaming as *alternating*, never *simultaneous*. Want me to fold this — "read_reply discards concurrent stream; no simultaneous transact+measure" — into that backlog item about frame-aware demux, so 0.3.0-6 explicitly confirms steps stay sequential and the discard is acceptable?

content.text:
Confirmed — your hypothesis is exactly right. Here's the lifecycle in `fc_2v3A0.py`, all in `run()` (line 222):

```
run():
  Sensor() + autoConnect           # 224-225  connect
  get_model()                      # 235      readRegister(Model)        ← register READ
  set_reference_frame_rotation()   # 236      readRegister + maybe        ← register READ/WRITE
                                   #          writeRegister/writeSettings/reset
  set_initial_angle()              # 239      setInitialHeading
  read_data()                      # 241      ← streaming loop starts here
```

**All register I/O happens in the three setup methods, before `read_data()`.** Once `read_data()` starts (line 342), the loop does only:

```python
cd = self.vs.getMostRecentMeasurement()   # 356 — nothing else touches registers
```

No `readRegister` / `writeRegister` anywhere in the loop (the only such calls in the whole file are lines 271, 298, 318 — all in setup). So once it's "flying," it never reads/writes a register again. You guessed it precisely.

## Two things that make this important

1. **fc.py uses the vnsdk, not rw-vn100** (line 33: `from vectornav import Sensor, Registers`). The flight data path is the SDK's, not our tool's. *We think* the SDK runs its own background reader thread that continuously parses the stream and keeps the latest `CompositeData` (that's what `getMostRecentMeasurement()` implies) — i.e. a *continuous parser that keeps the freshest sample*, not a "transact-or-measure" reader. So the SDK doesn't throw stream data away the way our `read_reply` does.

2. **That resolves the `read_reply` concern.** rw-vn100 is a **pre-flight setup/diagnostic tool**, not the in-flight data path. Its discard-during-transaction is fine *because the real flight scenario never reads a register mid-stream* — exactly your point. The "frame-aware demux for simultaneous transact+measure" worry I raised is therefore **low priority**: nothing in the actual flight workflow needs it. rw-vn100 configures and verifies before launch; the SDK streams during.

One setup detail worth flagging: `set_reference_frame_rotation` (261), when the matrix differs, does `writeRegister` → `writeSettings` → `reset` (298–300) — it **persists to flash and reboots** the device. That's the one heavyweight, power-cycle-level op, and it's firmly in setup, before any streaming. (It also means the ref-frame matrix is expected to *persist* across boots — relevant to the persist-vs-save/restore discussion earlier: this is a legitimate `--persist` use, a config that must survive reboot.)

So: nothing to fix in fc.py for the data-discard concern, and it down-ranks the demux item for rw-vn100. Want me to record this finding — "flight path is SDK + setup-before-stream, so rw-vn100 never needs simultaneous transact+measure" — as a short note in chores-01 (or the backlog item), so the rationale is captured rather than re-derived later?

96:- Cpp     : Fixed threading bugs in CommandProcessor causing dereferencing of already destructed commands
146:             - Updated ByteBuffer for improved thread-safety and added new methods to allow inputting to 
216: - Python  : Added thread to monitor and throw an exception if an AsyncError is received
225: - Cli     : Added thread to monitor and throw an exception if an AsyncError is received

content.text:
That's the definitive answer — and the SDK source confirms it cleanly. Your cross-check was right: there *is* threading, and it's exactly the demuxing architecture that means **no data is thrown away**. From `vnsdk/cpp/include/vectornav/Interface/Sensor.hpp`:

## The SDK runs a Listening Thread that demultiplexes the stream

- **`connect`/`autoConnect` starts a "Listening Thread"** when `THREADING_ENABLE` (Sensor.hpp:113, 118, 124, 131, 137); `disconnect` closes it (163). This is the background reader.
- **It routes packets by sync byte to dispatchers** (285–302): `AsciiPacketDispatcher`, **`FaPacketDispatcher`** (0xFA), **`FbPacketDispatcher`** (0xFB). `subscribeToMessage(queue, syncByte)` wires a queue to receive every matching measurement (290–293).
- **Measurements land in a `MeasurementQueue`** (173). `getMostRecentMeasurement()` "gets the back (most recent)... **popping every measurement in the queue**" (179–181) — that's the fc.py call.
- **Commands go through a separate `CommandProcessor`** (45, 252), blocking by default (`BlockWithRetry`, 192), with a non-blocking `sendCommand` API for the advanced case (238–246) — exactly the "blocking by default / non-blocking available" split you read in the docs.

## What this means for the "throwing data away" question

The Listening Thread reads **all** incoming bytes and sorts them: measurement packets → the queue, command replies → the CommandProcessor. So when fc.py issues a blocking `readRegister` *during* streaming, only the **calling thread** blocks — the Listening Thread keeps running and keeps filling the MeasurementQueue. **No stream data is dropped.**

This is precisely the "frame-aware demultiplexer" I described earlier as the bulletproof version — and the SDK actually implements it (dispatch by sync byte, including the 0xFB split packet that our rw-vn100 ignores).

## So the two tools sit at opposite design points

| | rw-vn100 `read_reply` | vnsdk Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Model | single-threaded, transact-**or**-measure | Listening Thread, transact-**and**-measure |
| Non-reply stream data during a transact | **discarded** | **demuxed into MeasurementQueue** |
| 0xFB split packets | ignored | handled (FbPacketDispatcher) |
| Role | pre-flight setup/diagnostics | in-flight data path |

And this **upgrades my earlier "we think"** about the SDK reader thread to *measured* — it's confirmed in the header, so it'd drop the speculation marker if recorded.

The bottom line your investigation establishes: in flight, data isn't thrown away because (a) fc.py does all register I/O before streaming, **and** (b) even if it didn't, the SDK's Listening Thread demuxes rather than discards. rw-vn100's discard is fine precisely because it's never the streaming data path — it's the setup tool you run *before* handing the link to the SDK.

Want me to capture this as a short architecture note (chores-01 or a `notes/` file) — "vnsdk = threaded Listening Thread + sync-byte dispatch + MeasurementQueue; rw-vn100 = single-threaded transact-or-measure; why the discard is acceptable" — so this whole investigation isn't re-derived later?

## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar

Commits: [[6]]

Today's `bench` is too complex: every run *mutates* the device
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and each subcommand is its own process — one port
open per invocation, and each fresh-process reopen is a wedge
die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
configuration into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, runs a whole
CLI line over a single connection, and adds file-backed named
states. We think running the line over one open cuts the
reconnect count that Issue #1 ties to wedges.

- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
  port once and runs its steps in order. See
  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
  output mode. See
  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).

### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps

The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
space handling.

- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
  single device write.
- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into

instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
  them to standalone / last.
- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.

### Transaction model: rw-vn100 discard vs the vnsdk Listening Thread

rw-vn100 and the vnsdk sit at opposite points on the question of
reading a register reply *while the device streams*. This matters
because the redesign's single-connection line (e.g.
`set-bin=accel,gyro+set-hz=200 bench`) reads registers on a link
that may already be streaming.

- **rw-vn100 is single-threaded — transact *or* measure.**
  `read_reply` (`src/transact.rs`) accumulates bytes to a newline,
  slices the candidate from the last `$`, and drops every line
  that isn't the matched reply — so any binary frames or async
  ASCII arriving *during* a register transaction are discarded.
- **The vnsdk is threaded — transact *and* measure.** `connect`
  starts a Listening Thread (vnsdk `Interface/Sensor.hpp`) that
  reads every byte and dispatches by sync byte (Ascii / Fa `0xFA`
  / Fb `0xFB` PacketDispatchers) into a MeasurementQueue;
  `getMostRecentMeasurement` pops it. Commands ride a separate
  CommandProcessor, blocking by default. A blocking register read
  parks only the caller — the Listening Thread keeps filling the
  queue, so no measurement is lost. This is measured from the
  header, not inferred.
- **Why rw-vn100's discard is acceptable.** rw-vn100 is the
  pre-flight setup / diagnostic tool, never the in-flight data
  path. The flight code (`../fc/src/fc_2v3A0.py`) does all
  register I/O in `run()` setup (`get_model`,
  `set_reference_frame_rotation`, `set_initial_angle`) *before*
  `read_data()`; once that loop starts it only calls
  `getMostRecentMeasurement()` — no register I/O mid-stream. And
  even if it did, the SDK demuxes rather than discards. So
  rw-vn100 never needs simultaneous transact+measure.
- **Out of scope, on purpose.** A frame-aware demultiplexer for
  rw-vn100 (route `0xFA`/`0xFB` to a frame counter, `$VN…` lines
  to the command matcher) is therefore *not* planned; the passive
  bench's measurement loop and the register steps stay sequential
  on the one connection. rw-vn100 also ignores `0xFB` split
  packets (the SDK handles them) — acceptable for the same reason;
  see REFERENCE.md "Framing & checksums" for that caveat.


## refactor: split main.rs into lib modules

ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.

### Transaction model: rw-vn100 discard vs the vnsdk Listening Thread

rw-vn100 and the vnsdk sit at opposite points on the question of
reading a register reply *while the device streams*. This matters
because the redesign's single-connection line (e.g.
`set-bin=accel,gyro+set-hz=200 bench`) reads registers on a link
that may already be streaming.

- **rw-vn100 is single-threaded — transact *or* measure.**
  `read_reply` (`src/transact.rs`) accumulates bytes to a newline,
  slices the candidate from the last `$`, and drops every line
  that isn't the matched reply — so any binary frames or async
  ASCII arriving *during* a register transaction are discarded.
- **The vnsdk is threaded — transact *and* measure.** `connect`
  starts a Listening Thread (vnsdk `Interface/Sensor.hpp`) that
  reads every byte and dispatches by sync byte (Ascii / Fa `0xFA`
  / Fb `0xFB` PacketDispatchers) into a MeasurementQueue;
  `getMostRecentMeasurement` pops it. Commands ride a separate
  CommandProcessor, blocking by default. A blocking register read
  parks only the caller — the Listening Thread keeps filling the
  queue, so no measurement is lost. This is measured from the
  header, not inferred.
- **Why rw-vn100's discard is acceptable.** rw-vn100 is the
  pre-flight setup / diagnostic tool, never the in-flight data
  path. The flight code (`../fc/src/fc_2v3A0.py`) does all
  register I/O in `run()` setup (`get_model`,
  `set_reference_frame_rotation`, `set_initial_angle`) *before*
  `read_data()`; once that loop starts it only calls
  `getMostRecentMeasurement()` — no register I/O mid-stream. And
  even if it did, the SDK demuxes rather than discards. So
  rw-vn100 never needs simultaneous transact+measure.
- **Out of scope, on purpose.** A frame-aware demultiplexer for
  rw-vn100 (route `0xFA`/`0xFB` to a frame counter, `$VN…` lines
  to the command matcher) is therefore *not* planned; the passive
  bench's measurement loop and the register steps stay sequential
  on the one connection. rw-vn100 also ignores `0xFB` split
  packets (the SDK handles them) — acceptable for the same reason;
  see REFERENCE.md "Framing & checksums" for that caveat.

input.content:
# rw-vn100 architecture

How `rw-vn100` is put together and the design decisions that aren't
obvious from the code. Protocol values (registers, framing, error
codes) live in [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md); this file is about the
tool's own structure and the *why* behind it.

## Module map

The crate is a thin binary over a library of focused modules
(`main.rs` → `lib.rs` → the rest):

- `main.rs` — thin entry point; parses args and calls into the lib.
- `lib.rs` — crate root and command dispatch (runs a parsed
  `Command` against an open port).
- `cli.rs` — arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and `--help` text.
- `proto.rs` — VN-100 protocol primitives: checksum/CRC, command
  framing, register parsers, and the `Field`/`FIELDS` table.
- `transact.rs` — port I/O: `read_reply`, `transact` /
  `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`.
- `bench.rs` — the `bench` command (measures the live stream).

## Transaction model: rw-vn100 discard vs the vnsdk Listening Thread

rw-vn100 and the VectorNav vnsdk sit at opposite points on one
question: can you read a register reply *while the device streams*?
It matters because the single-connection CLI line (e.g.
`set-bin=accel,gyro+set-hz=200 bench`) reads registers on a link
that may already be streaming.

- **rw-vn100 is single-threaded — transact *or* measure.**
  `read_reply` (`src/transact.rs`) accumulates bytes to a newline,
  slices the candidate from the last `$`, and drops every line
  that isn't the matched reply — so any binary frames or async
  ASCII arriving *during* a register transaction are discarded.
- **The vnsdk is threaded — transact *and* measure.** `connect`
  starts a Listening Thread (vnsdk `Interface/Sensor.hpp`) that
  reads every byte and dispatches by sync byte (Ascii / Fa `0xFA`
  / Fb `0xFB` PacketDispatchers) into a MeasurementQueue;
  `getMostRecentMeasurement` pops it. Commands ride a separate
  CommandProcessor, blocking by default. A blocking register read
  parks only the caller — the Listening Thread keeps filling the
  queue, so no measurement is lost. This is measured from the
  header, not inferred.
- **Why rw-vn100's discard is acceptable.** rw-vn100 is the
  pre-flight setup / diagnostic tool, never the in-flight data
  path. The flight code (`../fc/src/fc_2v3A0.py`) does all
  register I/O in `run()` setup (`get_model`,
  `set_reference_frame_rotation`, `set_initial_angle`) *before*
  `read_data()`; once that loop starts it only calls
  `getMostRecentMeasurement()` — no register I/O mid-stream. And
  even if it did, the SDK demuxes rather than discards. So
  rw-vn100 never needs simultaneous transact+measure.
- **Out of scope, on purpose.** A frame-aware demultiplexer for
  rw-vn100 (route `0xFA`/`0xFB` to a frame counter, `$VN…` lines
  to the command matcher) is therefore *not* planned; the passive
  bench's measurement loop and the register steps stay sequential
  on the one connection. rw-vn100 also ignores `0xFB` split
  packets (the SDK handles them) — acceptable for the same reason;
  see REFERENCE.md "Framing & checksums" for that caveat.

content.content:
1	# rw-vn100
2	
3	A small Rust CLI to read and configure a **VectorNav VN-100** IMU over a serial
4	port — and, just as importantly, a record of what we learned getting it to a
5	**reliable 200 Hz**.
6	
7	```
8	cargo run -- get-hz
9	cargo run -- set-hz 40 --persist
10	cargo run -- bench --bin --hz 200
11	```
12	
13	---
14	
15	## TL;DR — the headline finding
16	
17	The goal was 200 Hz of accelerometer data. The obvious path (crank the baud to
18	921600) turned out to be **the wrong one**. The right answer:
19	
20	> **Stay at the rock-solid 115200 baud and switch the device from its fat default
21	> ASCII message to a compact _binary_ output. 200 Hz then fits in under half the
22	> link, where 200 Hz of the default ASCII message is ~2× over.**
23	
24	`bench --bin` proves it on real hardware: **1000 frames in 5.00 s = 200.0 Hz**,
25	every frame CRC-valid, ~52 kbit/s of the ~115 kbit/s a 115200 line provides (~45%).
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Commands
30	
31	| Command | What it does |
32	|---|---|
33	| `get-hz` | Read the async output rate (register 7). |
34	| `set-hz <HZ> [--persist]` | Write the async output rate (validated). `--persist` saves to flash. |
35	| `baud <NEW> [--persist]` | Change the device serial baud (register 5), switch this connection to it, and verify — without closing the port. |
36	| `rrg <ID>` | Generic: read any register, print its fields. |
37	| `wrg <ID> <P1> [P2 …]` | Generic: write any register. Sharp tool — e.g. `wrg 5 921600` bypasses the safe baud switch; use `baud`. |
38	| `bench [--bin] [--hz HZ] [--secs S] [--fields LIST]` | Configure an output and **measure** the achieved rate, then restore. ASCII async by default; `--bin` selects a binary output (register 75); `--fields` picks the binary field set. |
39	| `reset` | Reboot the sensor (`$VNRST`); reloads saved flash settings. |
40	| `factory-reset` | Restore **all** registers to factory defaults and reboot (`$VNRFS`). Reverts to 115200 + default output. Not undoable. |
41	| `help` / `--help` / `-h` | Usage. |
42	
43	Binary `--fields` (Common group): `time, ypr, quat, gyro, accel, imu, magpres`.
44	
45	Global options: `--port PORT` (default `/dev/ttyUSB0`), `--baud BAUD` (default
46	`115200` — this is the rate the **host** talks at; it must match the device's
47	*current* rate).
48	
49	---
50	
51	## VN-100 protocol primer
52	
53	> For the **authoritative** register/enum/field values this tool relies on — with
54	> citations to the ICD (`../docs/`) and the vnsdk headers — see
55	> [`REFERENCE.md`](REFERENCE.md).
56	
57	**ASCII commands** look like `$<payload>*XX\r\n`, where `XX` is the 8-bit XOR
58	checksum of everything between `$` and `*`:
59	
60	```
61	Read register 7:    $VNRRG,07*74          -> $VNRRG,07,40*5C
62	Write register 7:   $VNWRG,07,40*59       -> $VNWRG,07,40*59
63	Write register 5:   $VNWRG,05,921600*53   (serial baud)
64	Write binary out:   $VNWRG,75,1,4,01,0101 (register 75, see below)
65	Save to flash:      $VNWNV*57             (writes ALL current registers)
66	Reboot:             $VNRST*4D
67	Factory reset:      $VNRFS*5F
68	Error reply:        $VNERR,<code>*XX
69	```
70

file.content:
# rw-vn100

A small Rust CLI to read and configure a **VectorNav VN-100** IMU over a serial
port — and, just as importantly, a record of what we learned getting it to a
**reliable 200 Hz**.

```
cargo run -- get-hz
cargo run -- set-hz 40 --persist
cargo run -- bench --bin --hz 200
```

---

## TL;DR — the headline finding

The goal was 200 Hz of accelerometer data. The obvious path (crank the baud to
921600) turned out to be **the wrong one**. The right answer:

> **Stay at the rock-solid 115200 baud and switch the device from its fat default
> ASCII message to a compact _binary_ output. 200 Hz then fits in under half the
> link, where 200 Hz of the default ASCII message is ~2× over.**

`bench --bin` proves it on real hardware: **1000 frames in 5.00 s = 200.0 Hz**,
every frame CRC-valid, ~52 kbit/s of the ~115 kbit/s a 115200 line provides (~45%).

---

## Commands

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `get-hz` | Read the async output rate (register 7). |
| `set-hz <HZ> [--persist]` | Write the async output rate (validated). `--persist` saves to flash. |
| `baud <NEW> [--persist]` | Change the device serial baud (register 5), switch this connection to it, and verify — without closing the port. |
| `rrg <ID>` | Generic: read any register, print its fields. |
| `wrg <ID> <P1> [P2 …]` | Generic: write any register. Sharp tool — e.g. `wrg 5 921600` bypasses the safe baud switch; use `baud`. |
| `bench [--bin] [--hz HZ] [--secs S] [--fields LIST]` | Configure an output and **measure** the achieved rate, then restore. ASCII async by default; `--bin` selects a binary output (register 75); `--fields` picks the binary field set. |
| `reset` | Reboot the sensor (`$VNRST`); reloads saved flash settings. |
| `factory-reset` | Restore **all** registers to factory defaults and reboot (`$VNRFS`). Reverts to 115200 + default output. Not undoable. |
| `help` / `--help` / `-h` | Usage. |

Binary `--fields` (Common group): `time, ypr, quat, gyro, accel, imu, magpres`.

Global options: `--port PORT` (default `/dev/ttyUSB0`), `--baud BAUD` (default
`115200` — this is the rate the **host** talks at; it must match the device's
*current* rate).

---

## VN-100 protocol primer

> For the **authoritative** register/enum/field values this tool relies on — with
> citations to the ICD (`../docs/`) and the vnsdk headers — see
> [`REFERENCE.md`](REFERENCE.md).

**ASCII commands** look like `$<payload>*XX\r\n`, where `XX` is the 8-bit XOR
checksum of everything between `$` and `*`:

```
Read register 7:    $VNRRG,07*74          -> $VNRRG,07,40*5C
Write register 7:   $VNWRG,07,40*59       -> $VNWRG,07,40*59
Write register 5:   $VNWRG,05,921600*53   (serial baud)
Write binary out:   $VNWRG,75,1,4,01,0101 (register 75, see below)
Save to flash:      $VNWNV*57             (writes ALL current registers)
Reboot:             $VNRST*4D
Factory reset:      $VNRFS*5F

file.content:
- Before: read reg 7 → write reg 75 → write reg 7=0. The fit check
  fired on the reg-75 write, before ASCII was silenced.
- After: read reg 7 → write reg 7=0 → write reg 75, and restore reg
  7 if the reg-75 write still fails — so a genuinely-too-big binary
  frame (e.g. the 38 B frame at 200 Hz / 115200, [[2]]) no longer
  leaves ASCII async switched off.
- `bench_ascii` is unaffected: it has a single stream and already
  writes the message type (reg 6) before the rate (reg 7).

## Rename rdwr_vn100 to rw-vn100

Commits: 

The renaming was because claude-code is converting the underscore to a
a hypen so we endup with two ~/.claude/projects and claude isn't using
our symlink to our /.claude repo.

So in our /.claude repo commit there are "new" .jsonl that were not
previously added.

input.description: Show vc-x1 push usage

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2-0

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate

content.text:
Now I see the real cause: `vc-x1` passes the body to `jj commit -m`, and `jj`'s parser rejects a body value that *starts* with `-`. Good news — preflight (fmt/clippy/test) passed, and vc-x1 cleanly **rolled both repos back** (no commit, no push, working copy intact). 

The fix is to start the body with a prose intro line (per prose-form, intro-then-bullets) so the `-m` value doesn't begin with `-`. Re-running with `--restart` to clear the failed run's saved state and use the new body. Same outcome intended — one docs commit, both repos, ochids added; I'll stop and wait after this.

input.command:
vc-x1 push main --restart --title="docs: fix ICD citations, add ARCHITECTURE.md" --body="Verification pass against the VN-100 ICD and User Manual, with the
device confirmed on firmware 3.1.0.0. Corrects mis-cited sections,
records which firmware/doc versions apply, and adds ARCHITECTURE.md.

- ARCHITECTURE.md: new — module map + transaction-model design
  (rw-vn100 transact-or-measure vs the vnsdk Listening Thread).
- README.md: protocol-primer callout also points to ARCHITECTURE.md.
- REFERENCE.md: framing cite §1.4 → §2.1.3 (format) / §1.4.2-3
  (checksums); new firmware-version provenance section (reg 1-4,
  device 3.1.0.0 = ICD); User Manual firmware-v1.1 caveat; sharpen
  reg 5/7/75, error, command cites; verified-date bump.
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: one-connection bullet points to
  ARCHITECTURE.md, add [9] ref.
- src/proto.rs: vn_crc16 cite → §2.1.3/§1.4.3; error_description → §1.5.
- src/cli.rs: VALID_RATES → §3.2.4; VALID_BAUDS → §3.2.2." --yes
input.description: Commit and push both repos via vc-x1 (body with prose intro)

lastPrompt: Use `vc-x1 push ..` as it will supply the commit message to both repos and add the ochid's
